text: Hello friends. This is a case of total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
This patient has DUB and she has the previous couple of caesarean section. So it was planned to do the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon begins using a bipolar device on the right upper pedicles during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
Just to show you that bipolar is also equally good to perform the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper to grasp the round ligament, which is located four centimeters lateral from the midline.
Bipolar Gasper is also very good and you can use the bipolar Robi Dissector also. That is a bipolar Maryland. Both the instruments are equally useful.

If you don't have a ligature or harmonic, you can use the total laparoscopic hysterectomy with the bipolar also. But care should be taken. That overcooking should be avoided. Bubbling of the water should be visible. And the color of the tissue should not go beyond the brown.
The surgeon completes the coagulation of the round ligament and proceeds to coagulate the fallopian tube using a bipolar device, ensuring proper hemostasis without overcooking the tissue.
The surgeon coagulates the fallopian tube, positioned approximately three centimeters lateral, using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon performs successive desiccation on the tissue using a bipolar instrument, followed by dissection with scissors. The coagulation process is visually evident, alternating between fulguration and desiccation modes.

The surgeon applies desiccation coagulation with firm contact and adequate pressure to seal elastin and collagen, causing the coagulated protein to constrict the vessel lumen. The major salpinx on the right side is visibly being separated.
The external iliac artery on the right side is visibly pulsating within the triangle of dome, demonstrating strong pulsation that is typically not observed.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesosalpinx and proceeds to cut it, ensuring proper separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon has dissected the mesosalpinx, revealing the ovarian ligament adjacent to the dissected area.
The surgeon positions near the ovary to minimize bleeding while coagulating the major ovarian ligament, which is visibly cooked to prevent reverse bleeding near the uterus.
The surgeon completes the previous step and proceeds to work on the mesovarian. They then move to the broad ligament, separating it up to 2.5 centimeters without detaching the peritoneum.
The surgeon completes a step involving the broad ligament, focusing on a section 2.5 centimeters over it.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the right side upper pedicle, preparing to move to the left side for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to coagulate the round ligament on the left side, visibly cooking the tissue to achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a cutting blade to quickly and efficiently cut the tissue, reducing instrumentation time compared to using bipolar forceps and scissors separately.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and scissors to cut the infundibulopelvic ligament, as the ovary on this side is planned for removal. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is then opened.

The assistant retroverts the uterus, pushing it towards the five o'clock position. The surgeon separates the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, progressively detaching the uterus from the surrounding structures between the five and seven o'clock positions.

The surgeon uses the left hand to separate and stretch the uterus, moving it from the five o'clock position to the midline and then towards the seven o'clock position, facilitating the dissection from one side to the opposite side.

The surgeon continues separating the peritoneum at the seven o'clock position, encountering adhesions with the bladder tissue. Blunt dissection is used to separate these adhesions, and if necessary, the tissue is lateralized from lateral to medial. A window is created using a harmonic device to aid in separation.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the bladder, revealing the vaginal fascia with a criss-cross pattern of vessels that are clearly visible and should be carefully separated.

The surgeon exposes the vaginal fascia, which is clearly visible, and then focuses on the posterior peritoneum. The uterus is positioned at two o'clock to facilitate separation of the left side of the posterior peritoneum, and then adjusted to ten o'clock for the right side.
The surgeon positions the uterus at the level of the arc of the uterosacral ligament. Using a ligature device, they coagulate the ascending uterine vessels before proceeding to the uterine artery.
The surgeon coagulates the right uterine artery without cutting it, following the technique of coagulating first before cutting.

The surgeon coagulates the left uterine artery, applying the ligature device to achieve hemostasis. After ensuring complete coagulation, the artery is cut, completing the separation of the left uterine artery.
The surgeon cuts the uterine artery, resulting in minor venous bleeding that is controlled by holding with a ligature. The uterine artery is visible medially, and further separation is performed.
The junction of the cervical vaginal fascia is positioned laterally. The left side dissection is completed, with the Macandrot partially cut.

The surgeon performs final coagulation and cutting near the cervix, ensuring the angles are coagulated to prevent bleeding during colpotomy. The procedure progresses to the right side, where the uterine artery has already been coagulated.
The surgeon makes the final cut on the right side, securing the uterine artery, which is visibly exposed. The procedure progresses to the initiation of colpotomy.

The surgeon secures the bladder and uses a full circle colpotomizer to cut the vagina, employing a harmonic device for precise dissection. The upper and posterior colpotomy is completed, followed by the right side using the harmonic tip. The uterus is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
The white colpotomizer is visible, indicating that the entire colpotomy has been completed. The uterus is then pulled through the vagina for removal.

The surgeon prepares to bisect the uterus if necessary, ensuring it can be removed easily through the vaginal canal. Once the uterus is extracted, the vaginal cuff is sutured to close the incision site.

The surgeon uses a fully curved needle to perform continuous suturing at the corner of the uterosacral ligament during a laparoscopic hysterectomy. The needle is held with stone holding forceps and passed through the vagina, with gloves and sponge packing to prevent gas leakage. The suturing progresses from right to left, terminating with a surgeon's knot using number one vicryl, assisted by an assistant to ensure the knot is secure.

The surgeon demonstrates tying a surgeon's knot with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps, using advanced energy sources to safely secure the uterine artery during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon completes the final wrap of the surgeon's knot and cuts the suture. The suture is then safely removed through a 5 mm port. Thorough irrigation and suction are performed to check for any bleeding, revealing clear fluid with no bleeding present. The surgical area is cleaned, and the procedure is concluded.
The surgeon performs repeated lavage to ensure the surgical area is clear of any bleeding, followed by final irrigation and suction to remove any remaining blood.
